title: Maximum Homerdrive

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Homer encounters something that goes wrong (inferred crash)
jokes.punchline: His signature catchphrase expressing frustration
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer catchphrase, perfectly economical and character-specific but not original at this point

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: So I said to that nurse... 'You can take your free tetanus shot and shove it.'
jokes.setup: Homer recounting what he told a nurse
jokes.punchline: Rejecting free medical care with unnecessary hostility
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer anti-logic - rejecting something beneficial out of stubbornness. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I told the nurse, 'I don't need your help! I'm fine! Get away from me with your stethoscope, you quack!'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Then she offered me a free checkup and I said, 'Free?! That's the most expensive thing I've ever heard!'

jokes.text: You still haven't told us why Lenny bit you.
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of workplace friend biting Homer
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy - one line that raises so many questions about what happened at work.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, your coworker called today.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Which one?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: The one who bit you.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: They all bit me, Marge.

jokes.text: Lousy meat-eating scum. - Huh? - Not you.
jokes.punchline: Homer naturally assumes it's about him, Lisa clarifies
jokes.explanation: Perfect character beat - Homer's guilty conscience makes him assume Lisa's vegetarian rant is about him.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You're nothing but meat-eating scum!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey! What'd I do?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Not you, Dad. I'm talking about the new football coach.

jokes.text: It's decorated with hanging steer carcasses and a fountain of blood.
jokes.punchline: Grotesquely over-the-top carnivorous decorations
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist restaurant concept, sets up the episode's location effectively.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: This restaurant is grotesquely over-the-top carnivorous. Look at these decorations!

jokes.text: Well, maybe the animals don't mind, honey. They might enjoy being the center of attention.
jokes.setup: Lisa describing animals being killed in front of diners
jokes.punchline: Homer's oblivious optimistic spin on animal slaughter
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic - turning animal execution into a positive experience about attention. Perfect character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, Lisa, maybe they should be flattered. I mean, who wouldn't want to spend their last moments in such a nice restaurant?

jokes.text: I think I read somewhere that cows like being killed.
jokes.setup: Continuing his defense of the restaurant
jokes.punchline: Claiming cows enjoy their own deaths
jokes.explanation: Peak Homer - making up 'facts' to justify what he wants to believe. Excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The cows enjoy their own deaths. It's like a big party for them.

jokes.text: Then why are we eating this crap?
jokes.punchline: Immediately insulting Marge's cooking
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer tactlessness - going from zero to insulting his wife's cooking instantly.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, this place is incredible! The food here is amazing!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I'm glad you're enjoying it, Homie.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, Marge, this makes me realize your cooking tastes like a tire fire.

jokes.text: You didn't need to knock the food on the floor. - Didn't I?
jokes.setup: Homer dramatically sweeping food off the table
jokes.punchline: Homer genuinely questioning if it was necessary
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer beat - he performs a dramatic gesture then genuinely questions its necessity. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Was that really necessary?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you just swept an entire roasted chicken onto the floor!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know, but did you have to make me feel bad about it?

jokes.text: Oh, whoa. A complimentary basket of hooves.
jokes.punchline: Serving animal hooves as bread replacement
jokes.explanation: Good visual absurdity - replacing bread basket with hooves. Homer's delighted reaction sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why would they serve hooves as a bread replacement?

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: It's our new artisanal approach to appetizers, sir.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Artisanal? This is just a hoof! I wanted bread, not something that belonged on a horse!

jokes.text: Mmm. Beef salad, beef on the cob, beef-fried beef-
jokes.punchline: Every dish is beef, including 'beef-fried beef'
jokes.explanation: Solid escalation joke building to the absurd 'beef-fried beef' - beef battered in beef.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Every dish is beef, including 'beef-fried beef'

jokes.text: Even this menu is made of meat. It's an entire chicken pounded flat.
jokes.setup: Explaining the restaurant's commitment to meat
jokes.punchline: The menu itself is a flattened chicken
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist detail - taking the meat theme to its logical extreme. Highly quotable and original.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: The menu itself is a flattened chicken

jokes.text: How clever. The kids' menu is on the beak.
jokes.setup: Learning the menu is a flattened chicken
jokes.punchline: Marge politely finding the bright side of grotesque menu design
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge reaction - finding something positive to say about something deeply disturbing. Great character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, at least it's a good source of protein.

jokes.text: On second thought, I'll just have a glass of milk... from that cow.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to avoid killing cows
jokes.punchline: Still results in cow execution just for milk
jokes.explanation: Great escalation payoff - even ordering milk results in immediate cow execution. Perfect dark comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm never killing another cow again. I'm going vegetarian!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That's wonderful, Homer!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, from now on I only drink milk.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, cows are killed for milk too.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: not a little girlie, underpantsie, pink-doily, tea-party place.
jokes.punchline: Increasingly ridiculous feminine descriptors
jokes.explanation: Decent escalating list but feels somewhat forced. 'Underpantsie' is a nice touch though.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: This steak is too small! I ordered a big juicy steak!

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Sir, that IS our largest steak.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well it's dainty. It's delicate. It's positively... petite. It's practically a ladylike cut of meat. Why, it's downright feminine! It's got a little purse! Look at these tiny, dainty garnishes!

jokes.text: We call it 'Sirloin-a-Lot.' It's the size of a boogie board.
jokes.punchline: Medieval pun name and absurd size comparison
jokes.explanation: Solid pun on 'Lancelot' and good absurdist size reference. Highly quotable name.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: We have our 'Sir Loin of Beef' — it's so big, you'll need a suit of armor to eat it.

jokes.text: Ooh! I'll have that one. And to drink, meatballs.
jokes.punchline: Ordering solid food as a drink
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer absurdity - treating meatballs as a beverage. Perfect economy and character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'd like to order your 64-ounce steak.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Sir, that comes as a entrée.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, I want it as a drink.

jokes.text: They like me because I'm brave.
jokes.setup: Crowd reacting to Homer's eating challenge
jokes.punchline: Homer's self-aggrandizing interpretation
jokes.explanation: Good Homer ego moment - misinterpreting spectacle as admiration for bravery.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! I'm a big, strong man!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: He ate 64 donuts!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's right, I did it for America.

jokes.text: It's you! You're him! You're Tony Randall!
jokes.punchline: Confusing him with the actor Tony Randall
jokes.explanation: Decent Homer confusion moment, though the Tony Randall reference is a bit dated/random.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, you're a famous trucker? Oh my God, you're Tony Randall!

dialogue.speaker: Trucker
dialogue.line: No, I'm not Tony Randall. I'm a truck driver.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sure, sure, whatever you say, Mr. Randall.

jokes.text: And I've eaten steaks from coast to coast with taters and toast.
jokes.punchline: Rhyming trucker speak about eating experience
jokes.explanation: Nice character voice establishment with folksy rhyming. Sets up Red's personality effectively.

dialogue.speaker: Red Barclay
dialogue.line: I've been drivin' trucks for thirty years, seen it all from coast to coast. I know the best diners, the worst rest stops, and everything in between.

dialogue.speaker: Red Barclay
dialogue.line: You want to know about eatin'? I'll tell you 'bout eatin'. I've had breakfast in Barstow, lunch in Las Vegas, dinner in Durango. Got the appetite of a rhino, got the taste buds of a pro. From the diner in Denver to the café in Chicago, I know where the good food go!

jokes.text: Greenhorn? Who's a greenhorn? What's a greenhorn?
jokes.punchline: Homer's confused escalating questions reveal he doesn't know what it means
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer beat - getting offended by an insult he doesn't understand. Good escalation structure.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What's a greenhorn? Is it someone who's green? Like, the Hulk? Am I the Hulk?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, does it mean I have a horn? A green horn? Where's my horn?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh! Is it like when Bart plays that green horn in band class? Am I Bart?!

jokes.text: It's an insult. Sock him, Dad. Sock everybody.
jokes.punchline: Bart escalating to violence against everyone
jokes.explanation: Good Bart escalation - going from targeted violence to indiscriminate violence. Nice economy.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: A greenhorn is a newbie, a rookie, someone who's new to the game.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: So I decided to test out my greenhorn status by punching Milhouse in the arm.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Then I moved on to Nelson, gave him a solid kick.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ralph didn't even see it coming when I tripped him down the stairs.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Even Principal Skinner got a piece of the action—a slingshot to the back of the head.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: By the end of the day, I'd basically assaulted every kid in Springfield and half the faculty.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Turns out being a greenhorn is way more fun than I thought.

jokes.text: Mr. Hasn't-Got-A-Belly.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to insult Red back
jokes.punchline: Childish, redundant insult about belly size
jokes.explanation: Homer's weak attempt at comeback insults. The redundancy and childishness are funny.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well, your belly is so big, your belly is so big!

jokes.text: You know, I wouldn't have thought so before I bought 12% percent of this restaurant... but now I feel a balanced diet can include the occasional eating contest.
jokes.setup: Asked if eating contest is safe
jokes.punchline: Dr. Hibbert's medical opinion changes based on financial interest
jokes.explanation: Excellent satire of medical ethics and conflicts of interest. Dr. Hibbert's corruption is played perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, it's perfectly safe. I'm the team physician.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: On second thought, competitive eating is extremely dangerous. That'll be $150.

jokes.text: We have the latest Heimlich machine. [Gagging]
jokes.punchline: Automated Heimlich device that appears to be malfunctioning/violent
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag about mechanizing first aid with questionable results.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Now, if your child is choking, simply press this button for an automated Heimlich maneuver.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: It's completely safe.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What could possibly go wrong?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well...

jokes.text: And on your right, Homer somebody.
jokes.punchline: Homer gets no respect, not even his last name
jokes.explanation: Nice understated put-down of Homer as nobody special. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: Introducing the eating contest participants: In lane one, we have Champion Eater Joey Chestnut!

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: In lane two, we have Competitive Eater Takeru Kobayashi!

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: And in lane three... Homer... uh... Simpson? No wait, just... Homer.

jokes.text: No, Homer! Don't fill up on bread!
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately makes rookie mistake of eating bread
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer incompetence - making the most basic error immediately. Marge as voice of reason.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... bread.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, no! You're supposed to save room for the actual food!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But Marge, bread IS food.

jokes.text: Must eat beef. Must defeat guy I just met.
jokes.punchline: Reducing complex situation to simple imperatives, noting the arbitrariness
jokes.explanation: Excellent Homer internal voice - caveman logic plus acknowledgment of the arbitrary nature of his rivalry.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Okay Homer, think. You're in a contest. You need to win. First prize is a boat. If you win the boat, you get a boat. Second prize is a jet ski. If you lose, you don't get a boat. The key to winning is... not losing.

jokes.text: Lungs filling. Sinuses packed with meat.
jokes.punchline: Grotesque medical description of meat overconsumption
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny medical detail about extreme overeating. Good visceral imagery.

jokes.text: He's not even halfway through Walter and he's already hallucinating.
jokes.punchline: Lisa casually naming the cow Homer is eating
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - giving the steak a human name makes it disturbing and funny. Great Lisa character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, that's Bessie.

jokes.text: There's still food, but I don't want to eat it. I've become everything I've ever hated.
jokes.punchline: Dramatic existential crisis about not wanting food
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character moment - treating not wanting food as a profound personal failure. Excellent character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm at my physical limit. I can't eat another bite.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait... did I just say I don't want food? What's happening to me? Am I dying? Is this what death feels like?

jokes.text: This man is dead. Looks to me like beef poisoning. - Probably from some other restaurant.
jokes.setup: Red Barclay dies from eating contest
jokes.punchline: Dr. Hibbert immediately deflects blame from his restaurant investment
jokes.explanation: Great callback to his conflict of interest. Even diagnosing death, he protects his investment.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, it appears Red Barclay has died from acute gastrointestinal distress due to competitive eating. However, I should note that my restaurant investment in this establishment was completely separate from my medical practice, so I bear no responsibility whatsoever.

jokes.text: He called me greenhorn. I called him Tony Randall. It was a thing we had.
jokes.setup: Homer reflecting on his relationship with Red
jokes.punchline: Treating mutual confusion/insults as a meaningful bond
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that reframes their brief interaction as meaningful. Perfect Homer sentimentality.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, Red and I have been through a lot together. We've insulted each other, confused each other, and basically had no idea what the other one was talking about most of the time.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But that's what makes our bond so special. We're connected by a deep, meaningful relationship built on mutual incomprehension and constant bickering.

jokes.text: I owe it to old Red as both his friend and his killer.
jokes.setup: Homer deciding to complete Red's delivery
jokes.punchline: Casually acknowledging he caused Red's death
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - Homer's matter-of-fact acknowledgment of being both friend and killer. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, Red's not coming back, so I guess I'll just finish his delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I probably should've checked if he was still in the truck bed before I drove off that cliff.

jokes.text: Don't you have school? - Don't you have work? - Ah, touche!
jokes.punchline: Bart turning it back on Homer, who admits defeat
jokes.explanation: Nice setup-punchline structure with good economy. Homer's 'touche' acknowledgment is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Bart, why are you wasting your time playing video games instead of doing your homework?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Why are you wasting your time watching TV instead of going to the gym?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: That didn't sound like you meant it. - Oh, all right. Good-bye, sweetheart. Have a nice trip. - That's more like it.
jokes.punchline: Homer coaching her to be more affectionate
jokes.explanation: Good relationship comedy - Homer demanding proper emotional send-off. Nice character dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bye, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, Marge, Marge. You gotta put more love in it. Try again.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bye, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, no, no. With feeling! Like you're really gonna miss me.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bye, Homer. I love you.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's better. Now get out of here before I cry.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah? My left arm says different. - You're not really a trucker now.
jokes.setup: Homer claiming to be a trucker
jokes.punchline: Homer's trucker tan as 'evidence', Bart's reality check
jokes.explanation: Decent physical comedy about trucker tans, though not particularly original.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look at me, boy! I'm a real trucker now. See this tan? This is what years on the open road does to a man.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you got that tan sitting in the driveway for three hours with the window down.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly! The open road, Bart. The open road.

jokes.text: No, Dad! He wants you to blow your horn. - Oh. [Homer] That little punk!
jokes.punchline: Homer misinterprets gesture as insult, then complies and still insults kid
jokes.explanation: Standard road rage comedy with Homer's predictable overreaction to perceived slight.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Honk if you love donuts!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's not a love gesture, that's an insult! Why you little...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: HONK HONK!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And stay off my lawn, ya punk!

jokes.text: They've eaten submarine sandwiches, grinders and hoagies.
jokes.setup: Lisa reading postcards from Homer and Bart
jokes.punchline: Different regional names for the same sandwich
jokes.explanation: Nice observational humor about regional sandwich terminology. Lisa's earnest delivery sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh, look. Postcards from Dad and Bart. Let me see what they have to say.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad writes: 'Dear Lisa, I'm in Philly. Just had the most amazing sandwich. It's called a hoagie.'

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: And Bart writes: 'Yo Lis, I'm in New Orleans. Had this awesome sandwich called a po'boy.'

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: And here's one from Dad again: 'Now I'm in Connecticut. They call it a grinder here.'

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: This is fascinating. The same sandwich, called by completely different names depending on the region.

jokes.text: We're buying a new doorbell? - A musical doorbell.
jokes.setup: Marge's idea of walking on the wild side
jokes.punchline: Extremely tame rebellion of buying musical doorbell
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge character moment - her version of rebellion is hilariously mundane. Great character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I'm going to walk on the wild side and buy a musical doorbell.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom, that's not walking on the wild side, that's just... suburban.

jokes.text: So many doorbells. I'm in way over my head.
jokes.punchline: Overwhelmed by simple shopping decision
jokes.explanation: Good Marge anxiety moment - treating trivial choice as major life decision. Solid character work.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: How many dings does a doorbell really need? One? Two? Do I go with the classic ding-dong or something more contemporary like the ding-ding-ding?

jokes.text: We got your ding-dang-dongs and your do-re-mis and your ha-cha-chas, huh?
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous made-up doorbell categories with musical/sound names
jokes.explanation: Good character voice with silly doorbell categorization. 'Ha-cha-chas' is a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Senor Ding-Dong
dialogue.line: We have the classic ding-dong, the musical ding-dong, the Westminster chimes model, the cuckoo bird special, and our deluxe symphony edition that plays the entire first movement of Beethoven's Fifth.

jokes.text: Lisa, you ought to be a doorbell salesman. - Oh, that's just what I need. Another piranha in the tank.
jokes.setup: Marge praising Lisa's doorbell finding ability
jokes.punchline: Senor Ding-Dong's cutthroat view of doorbell sales
jokes.explanation: Good character reaction - treating doorbell sales as incredibly competitive business. Nice economy.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Lisa, you have such a gift for finding doorbells.

dialogue.speaker: Senor Ding-Dong
dialogue.line: Yes, and in the doorbell business, we call that a threat.

jokes.text: Great. Now write your mother and tell her the marriage is off. And then when the paperwork is done, I'll make Gwen my wife.
jokes.punchline: Immediately planning to divorce Marge for waitress
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer impulsiveness - ready to end marriage based on friendly waitress service.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm, truck stop coffee. And look at this waitress! She's got the triple-stacked pie rack going on.

dialogue.speaker: Waitress
dialogue.line: More coffee, hon?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yes! And I'm divorcing my wife. We can run away together!

jokes.text: Oh! Milhouse is selling seeds, and he's coming this way- Oh, the birds got him.
jokes.setup: Lisa excited about visitor for doorbell test
jokes.punchline: Milhouse immediately attacked by birds offscreen
jokes.explanation: Great visual comedy - instant reversal from hope to disaster. Excellent economy and timing.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh boy, we finally have a visitor for my doorbell test!

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Hi Lisa!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Perfect! Now let me document your reaction to the doorbell.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Sure, I'd be happy to—AAAAAHHHHH!

jokes.text: What if we don't have all the answers? - You're right, Nureen. Let's go get real jobs.
jokes.punchline: Having existential crisis and abandoning faith mission
jokes.explanation: Good ironic reversal - religious missionaries having crisis of faith at worst possible moment.

dialogue.speaker: Jehovah's Witness #1
dialogue.line: Good morning. We're here to spread the word of—

dialogue.speaker: Jehovah's Witness #2
dialogue.line: Wait. Why are we even doing this? What if we're wrong?

dialogue.speaker: Jehovah's Witness #1
dialogue.line: What do you mean, wrong? This is our faith.

dialogue.speaker: Jehovah's Witness #2
dialogue.line: But how do we know? How does anyone know? Are we just going door to door because our parents did?

dialogue.speaker: Jehovah's Witness #1
dialogue.line: I... I never thought about it that way.

dialogue.speaker: Jehovah's Witness #2
dialogue.line: I'm done. I'm going back to college.

dialogue.speaker: Jehovah's Witness #1
dialogue.line: Wait for me.

jokes.text: Oh. I would have feigned interest.
jokes.punchline: Marge admits she would have been politely dishonest
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge character beat about Minnesota nice behavior

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, they seemed like such nice people. I would have told them what they wanted to hear.

jokes.text: It's time we opened up a can of Whup-Tushie on this situation.
jokes.setup: Marge needs to solve the doorbell situation
jokes.punchline: Her attempt at tough talk with euphemistic language
jokes.explanation: Good Marge character moment trying to be tough but staying clean

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Listen here, you little ankle-biters. I don't know who you are, but if you ring that doorbell one more time, I'm going to come out there and give you such a pinch!

jokes.text: Ring the bell! - Why? You already know I'm here, don't you? - Just do it! - Nothing doin', missy.
jokes.punchline: Luigi's logical refusal to perform pointless action
jokes.explanation: Good frustration comedy - Marge's obsession meeting practical resistance.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Please, Luigi, just ring the doorbell!

dialogue.speaker: Luigi
dialogue.line: But why? There's nobody inside to answer it.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I just need to know if it works!

dialogue.speaker: Luigi
dialogue.line: It's a doorbell, Marge. Of course it works. That's what doorbells do.

jokes.text: Doorbell playing song repeatedly
jokes.punchline: Song plays over and over, becoming annoying
jokes.explanation: Standard escalation gag - wish fulfillment becoming nightmare. Predictable but functional.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Finally, we can test the new doorbell!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: What song did you choose, Mom?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh, just a lovely classical piece.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: ♪ La la la la ♪

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: There, that's nice and—

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom, it's been playing for ten minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: ♪ La la la la ♪

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Make it stop! Make it stop!

jokes.text: Actually, we have to go 2,200 miles in 10 hours.
jokes.setup: Homer thinks they're ahead of schedule
jokes.punchline: Bart reveals they're impossibly behind
jokes.explanation: Standard revelation that things are worse than thought

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Great news, everyone! We're way ahead of schedule!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, we're not ahead of schedule. We're impossibly behind.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's impossible! How can we be behind if we're ahead?

jokes.text: Well, Congress is racing back to Washington to outlaw these.
jokes.punchline: Pills so dangerous Congress is rushing to ban them
jokes.explanation: Good satire of dangerous products and regulatory response. Nice economy and dark edge.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Do you have any stimulants?

dialogue.speaker: Store Clerk
dialogue.line: Pills so dangerous Congress is rushing to ban them.

jokes.text: No problem. I'll balance it out with a bottle of sleeping pills.
jokes.setup: Being warned about taking too many pep pills
jokes.punchline: Homer's solution is to add opposite drugs
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - treating dangerous drug interaction as simple balancing act.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: If I'm taking too many pep pills, I'll just balance it out with downers. Problem solved!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's like a seesaw, but inside my body.

jokes.text: Pep pills! Perking up again! I could drive all... night. [Snoring]
jokes.setup: Homer cycling between stimulants and depressants
jokes.punchline: Falls asleep mid-sentence about staying awake
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy with nice timing - falling asleep while declaring ability to stay awake.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I gotta stay awake. I gotta stay awake. I gotta stay aw... zzzzz

jokes.text: I fell asleep at the wheel, and the truck drove here by itself.
jokes.setup: Homer waking up after sleeping while driving
jokes.punchline: Attributing safe arrival to miracle rather than technology
jokes.explanation: Sets up the auto-drive reveal nicely. Homer's miracle interpretation is character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! We made it! It's a miracle!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you were on cruise control.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Praise Jesus!

jokes.text: all you gotta do is sit back and feel your ass grow.
jokes.setup: Explaining how easy auto-drive makes trucking
jokes.punchline: Blunt description of job's physical effects
jokes.explanation: Good blue-collar humor about job's downsides. Nice economy and quotable phrase.

dialogue.speaker: Trucker
dialogue.line: Yeah, with auto-drive, this job's a breeze. I just sit here and watch the road.

dialogue.speaker: Trucker
dialogue.line: Only downside is my back's shot, my neck's all stiff, and I can't feel my legs anymore.

jokes.text: Wow! You guys are even lazier than me.
jokes.punchline: Homer impressed by level of laziness exceeding his own
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character moment - finding people lazier than him genuinely impressive.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, those truckers are lazier than me? That's impossible!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm in awe. I've met my master.

jokes.text: Come on out, boy! It's windy!
jokes.setup: Homer demonstrating auto-drive by hanging out window
jokes.punchline: Inviting Bart to join dangerous behavior
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy. Homer's casual attitude toward danger is character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey Bart, come on out here! It's amazing!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you're hanging out the window while the car's moving!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know! That's what makes it fun! Come on, live a little!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: No way, man!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Aw, you're no fun. Hey, at least Maggie's got some guts!

jokes.text: But keep it a secret. It's a big scam, okay?
jokes.setup: Homer telling crowd about auto-drive system
jokes.punchline: Immediately betraying truckers' secret to large audience
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer obliviousness - revealing secret while asking people to keep it secret.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey everybody, check out this amazing auto-drive system! It's so advanced, the truckers have been using it for years to stay awake on long hauls.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: They told me it was their secret weapon, but I figured, why keep it to myself? You all deserve to know!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's just a little... *whispers loudly* ...methamphetamine!

jokes.text: Yeah. Just like we did to Jimmy Hoffa. - Hey, shut up!
jokes.setup: Truckers planning to deal with Homer
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to murdering famous missing person
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy reference to real mystery. Good economy and timing with the shutup.

jokes.text: Oh, the heck with it. [Wire cutters snapping]
jokes.setup: Marge can't find tools to cut doorbell wire
jokes.punchline: Uses wire cutters directly on wires in frustration
jokes.explanation: Decent escalation of Marge's frustration, though the solution seems obvious.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh, why can't I find the wire cutters?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I'll just use these wire cutters directly on the wires!

jokes.text: They're hazing us to initiate us into the truckers' fraternity. Thank you, sir! May I have another?
jokes.punchline: Homer misinterprets violence as friendly hazing
jokes.explanation: Good Homer optimism in the face of obvious danger

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, these guys sure are friendly! They're really giving us the old college try!

dialogue.speaker: Trucker
dialogue.line: We're trying to kill you, you idiot!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ha ha! That's a good one! You almost had me there!

jokes.text: Oh! My good knife! My wife's gonna kill me.
jokes.punchline: More worried about domestic consequences than current danger
jokes.explanation: Good priority joke - fearing wife more than current violence. Nice character detail.

dialogue.speaker: Trucker
dialogue.line: A massive explosion! A gun fight! A high-speed chase! But when I get home, my wife is gonna kill me for missing dinner!

jokes.text: I'm keeping a promise to an American roadmaster. Red! The trucker!
jokes.setup: Homer explains his motivation for the dangerous jump
jokes.punchline: His heroic language for a brief acquaintance
jokes.explanation: Good Homer romanticizing a stranger he barely knew

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm doing this for Lenny. We've been best friends for... how long has it been?

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: About twenty years.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I had no idea! I thought it was like three weeks.

jokes.text: I'm not Red. I'm Homer. - Gotta go!
jokes.setup: Auto-drive system trying to stop Homer
jokes.punchline: Homer's identity correction followed by immediate dismissal
jokes.explanation: Decent human-computer interaction comedy. Homer's abrupt departure is characteristic.

dialogue.speaker: Computer
dialogue.line: You are not Homer Simpson.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I am too Homer Simpson!

dialogue.speaker: Computer
dialogue.line: Identity confirmed. Homer Simpson, you are under arrest.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Now that man is a genuine, steel-belted, gear-jamming rig jockey.
jokes.punchline: Elaborate trucker jargon praise
jokes.explanation: Good trucker dialect with creative metaphors. 'Steel-belted' is a nice tire reference.

dialogue.speaker: Trucker 1
dialogue.line: Did you see that? That's the best driving I've ever seen!

dialogue.speaker: Trucker 2
dialogue.line: Yeah, man, he's got the rubber side down, the shiny side up!

dialogue.speaker: Trucker 1
dialogue.line: He's ten-four, good buddy. That's one smooth operator.

dialogue.speaker: Trucker 2
dialogue.line: Smokey's gonna want to give him a medal for that lane change!

jokes.text: Nah. Let's just find some other scam. - Hey, how about bootlegging Beanie Babies?
jokes.punchline: Immediately pivoting to another scam with timely reference
jokes.explanation: Good period reference to Beanie Baby craze. Shows truckers' commitment to avoiding honest work.

dialogue.speaker: Trucker 1
dialogue.line: We don't do honest work.

dialogue.speaker: Trucker 2
dialogue.line: Yeah, we're moving to cryptocurrency scams instead.

dialogue.speaker: Trucker 1
dialogue.line: NFTs, baby!

jokes.text: Somewhere up there, I bet Red is saying thanks. 10-4, dead buddy.
jokes.setup: Bart suggests Red is grateful in heaven
jokes.punchline: Homer's radio response acknowledges Red's death
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor with trucker radio protocol

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Maybe Red's grateful up in heaven.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Red's dead?

jokes.text: Artichokes and migrant workers. Looking good.
jokes.punchline: Casually listing human beings as cargo
jokes.explanation: Dark immigration commentary with excellent economy. Treating people as equivalent to vegetables.

dialogue.speaker: Dock Worker
dialogue.line: Let's see here... we got a shipment of ball bearings, ten dozen boxes of some kinda raw material, and... uh... oh yeah, a couple of people in the back.

jokes.text: Well, the last time I saw him, he was in a big plastic bag.
jokes.punchline: Homer's matter-of-fact description of Red's corpse
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy with Homer's casual attitude toward death. Nice economy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Red? Oh, he's dead. Been dead for three weeks. He's in the freezer next to the TV dinners.

jokes.text: Are you crazy? I'm not driving a trainload of napalm to Springfield!
jokes.setup: Homer and Bart needing transportation home
jokes.punchline: Extremely dangerous cargo being transported to their hometown
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and dark edge. Napalm to Springfield raises questions about the town.

dialogue.speaker: Train Engineer
dialogue.line: Well, you can ride in the caboose, but I gotta warn ya - we're carrying a full load of nitroglycerine, napalm, and Dennis Rodman.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dennis Rodman?!

dialogue.speaker: Train Engineer
dialogue.line: Yeah, he's the most dangerous of all.

jokes.text: Huh? - Ayayay! Senor Ding-Dong!
jokes.punchline: Senor Ding-Dong materializing from the doorbell itself
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist reveal - marketing character becoming literally real. Good visual surprise.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: What is this thing?

dialogue.speaker: Senor Ding-Dong
dialogue.line: ¡Hola, amigos! I am Senor Ding-Dong!

jokes.text: There was a time when that was true. But now I am so much more.
jokes.setup: Homer comments on the mascot being fake
jokes.punchline: Senor Ding-Dong has transcended his commercial origins
jokes.explanation: Decent philosophical moment for cartoon mascot

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, you mean Senor Ding-Dong isn't real?

dialogue.speaker: Senor Ding-Dong
dialogue.line: I have transcended my commercial origins, Homer. I am more than just a mascot now.

jokes.text: There was a time when that was true. But now I am so much more.
jokes.setup: Marge saying he's just a marketing gimmick
jokes.punchline: Senor Ding-Dong's existential evolution beyond marketing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary on corporate mascots gaining sentience. Philosophical depth in absurd context.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: He's just a marketing gimmick.

dialogue.speaker: Senor Ding-Dong
dialogue.line: Marketing gimmick? I am more than that. I have transcended my commercial origins. I am a being of pure consciousness, existing beyond the constraints of advertising.

jokes.text: If you ever need me, just ring.
jokes.punchline: Perfect pun on his doorbell nature and offer to help
jokes.explanation: Perfect pun that ties character to function. Great economy and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Senor Ding-Dong
dialogue.line: If you need me again, just ring my bell!

jokes.text: Does anyone have any jumper cables? [Engine Cranks, Stops] [Senor Ding-Dong] Oh, you stinking Chevy!
jokes.setup: Homer needs help with car trouble
jokes.punchline: Even the magical mascot has car problems
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of magical character with mundane car trouble

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My car won't start. I need help!

dialogue.speaker: Señor Ding-Dong
dialogue.line: Even I have car trouble, amigo. My enchanted vehicle, she no start either.

jokes.text: Oh, you stinking Chevy!
jokes.punchline: Car won't start, ruining his mystical departure
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of mystical character moment with mundane car trouble. Excellent structure and timing.

dialogue.speaker: Senor Ding-Dong
dialogue.line: I shall disappear into the night, never to be seen again!

dialogue.speaker: Senor Ding-Dong
dialogue.line: What? The car won't start? No! This is not how the mystical arts work!

tmdb_overview: After beating Homer in a steak-eating competition, a trucker dies; Homer and Bart take over his rig to complete his shipment.
insight: Homer's truck-driving odyssey delivers 77 jokes but lands below peak Simpsons—solid character work, weak punchline payoff.
